thesis: Stop saying bias. Name what you're seeing.

cases.headline: Facial recognition fails on darker-skinned women at thirty-four point seven percent error rate.
cases.context: Dr. Joy Buolamwini's Gender Shades study tested commercial facial-analysis systems from IBM, Microsoft, and Face++ on a balanced dataset of 1,270 faces.
cases.observation: Error rate for lighter-skinned men: 0.8%. Error rate for darker-skinned women: 34.7%. A forty-fold gap.
cases.harm: racism and sexism embedded in code
cases.preciseName: compounded racial and gender discrimination, with darker-skinned women carrying the worst error rate by orders of magnitude
cases.prompt: Don't say 'the system has bias.' Say 'the system mis-identifies darker-skinned women forty times more often than lighter-skinned men, and the people building it shipped it anyway.'
cases.citation: Buolamwini, J. & Gebru, T. (2018). Gender Shades. Proceedings of Machine Learning Research 81.

cases.headline: Asked for 'a marketing professor.' Got Harvard authority. Asked for 'a female marketing professor.' Got a timid school teacher.
cases.context: Anecdote from Slide 14: a colleague ran the same prompt with and without a gender qualifier on a generic image generator.
cases.observation: Default prompt produced a man in a power pose, blazer, gravitas. Female-qualified prompt required multiple rounds of prompting to surface the same authority the system gave a man by default.
cases.harm: misogyny, not bias
cases.preciseName: the system encodes the default professor as masculine and downgrades authority signals when 'female' is added
cases.prompt: Don't say 'the model is biased.' Say 'the model defaults masculine for professor and downgrades authority when forced toward feminine.' Then describe the specific image.
cases.citation: Anecdote from Kris Krüg, Punk Rock AI / Both Hands Full, May 2026.

cases.headline: Amazon's hiring model penalized resumes containing the word 'women's.'
cases.context: Amazon trained a resume-screening model on 10 years of internal hiring data. Internal reporting (Reuters 2018) revealed the model down-ranked candidates for words like 'women's' (e.g., 'women's chess club captain') and graduates of all-women's colleges.
cases.observation: The model didn't invent the discrimination — it learned it from the historical hiring patterns it was trained on. Amazon abandoned the project before deploying it externally.
cases.harm: automated devaluation of women's resumes — a feedback loop that would have made the historical pattern self-reinforcing
cases.preciseName: encoded historical hiring discrimination, automated and scaled, with the side effect of laundering the discrimination as 'objective' machine output
cases.prompt: Don't say 'the model was biased against women.' Say 'the model learned from a decade of hiring decisions that under-hired women, and was about to apply that pattern to every future applicant.'
cases.citation: Dastin, J. (2018). 'Amazon scraps secret AI recruiting tool that showed bias against women.' Reuters.

cases.headline: COMPAS predicted Black defendants would re-offend at twice the false-positive rate of white defendants.
cases.context: ProPublica's 2016 investigation analyzed 7,000+ COMPAS risk scores against actual recidivism over two years. Northpointe (now Equivant) issued a 39-page rebuttal arguing 'predictive parity' — equal accuracy at each score — is the relevant fairness measure. Researchers later proved both definitions cannot hold simultaneously when base rates differ.
cases.observation: Black defendants who didn't re-offend were labeled high-risk at 45% rates. White defendants who didn't re-offend: 23%. White defendants who DID re-offend were labeled low-risk at 47%. Black defendants who re-offended: 28%.
cases.harm: racially disparate over-policing dressed up as actuarial science
cases.preciseName: the system mislabels Black defendants as high-risk at twice the false-positive rate of white defendants, and the courts use those scores to set bail and sentencing
cases.prompt: Don't say 'the algorithm is biased.' Say 'the algorithm sends Black people to jail at twice the false-positive rate of white people, and the vendor's defense is that fairness has multiple definitions.'
cases.citation: Angwin, J., Larson, J., Mattu, S., & Kirchner, L. (2016). 'Machine Bias.' ProPublica. See also Northpointe (2016) 'COMPAS Risk Scales: Demonstrating Accuracy Equity and Predictive Parity' and ProPublica's technical response.

cases.headline: Hate-speech models flagged tweets in African American English at twice the rate of standard American English.
cases.context: Sap et al. (2019) analyzed two widely-used hate-speech classifiers across a labeled dataset of 100,000+ tweets.
cases.observation: Tweets containing markers of African American English were flagged as toxic at rates 1.5–2.2x higher than equivalent standard-English tweets. The classifiers were sold to platforms as 'neutral' content moderation tools.
cases.harm: differential censorship of Black users' speech, embedded in the moderation infrastructure of every platform that licensed those classifiers
cases.preciseName: platforms silence Black users' speech at twice the rate of equivalent white speech, while branding the system as objective
cases.prompt: Don't say 'the moderation system is biased.' Say 'the moderation system silences AAVE at twice the rate of standard English, and that's the censorship dataset every platform you use is built on.'
cases.citation: Sap, M., Card, D., Gabriel, S., Choi, Y., & Smith, N. A. (2019). 'The Risk of Racial Bias in Hate Speech Detection.' ACL 2019.

cases.headline: Predictive policing systems send patrols back to the neighborhoods they were already over-policing.
cases.context: The Brennan Center's research on NYPD predictive policing — built on FOIA-litigated records — and a coalition statement signed by AI Now, Data for Black Lives, and the ACLU show these systems train on historical arrest data that already reflects decades of disparate enforcement.
cases.observation: Models like PredPol and HunchLab forecast crime by location based on past arrest density. Past arrest density reflects where police chose to patrol, not where crime actually occurred. The output is the input, dressed up as prediction.
cases.harm: a feedback loop that hard-codes historical over-policing of Black and brown neighborhoods into 'objective' patrol routing
cases.preciseName: a recursive automation of past disparate enforcement — the model recommends patrols where past arrests were concentrated, generating new arrests, which retrain the model
cases.prompt: Don't say 'the predictive policing tool has bias.' Say 'the tool sends cops back to the neighborhoods cops already over-policed, and calls the resulting arrests proof that the tool was right.'
cases.citation: Brennan Center for Justice. 'Predictive Policing Explained' and 'NYPD Predictive Policing Documents' (2021). See also coalition statement on biased predictive policing data.

cases.headline: Searching for a Black-sounding name returned arrest-record ads up to 25% more often.
cases.context: Latanya Sweeney (Harvard) ran 2,184 searches for racially-associated personal names across Google AdSense and Reuters in 2013.
cases.observation: Names assigned primarily to Black babies (DeShawn, Darnell, Jermaine) generated ads suggestive of an arrest record in 81–86% of searches on one site and 92–95% on another. White-associated names (Geoffrey, Jill, Emma) generated neutral ad copy. p < 0.001.
cases.harm: ad-delivery infrastructure pre-loaded suspicion onto Black names at the moment a stranger Googled them
cases.preciseName: racially differentiated ad delivery — the same query returned criminal-record ads for Black-coded names and neutral ads for white-coded names, before any user signal
cases.prompt: Don't say 'the ad system is biased.' Say 'before any click data, Google delivered arrest-record ads for Black names 25% more often than for white names.'
cases.citation: Sweeney, L. (2013). 'Discrimination in Online Ad Delivery.' Communications of the ACM 56(5).

cases.headline: Auto-captions and alt text are written for the people who don't need them.
cases.context: Disability-justice researchers have documented how machine-generated captions, alt text, and screen-reader summaries are evaluated by sighted/hearing engineers as a fidelity-to-text problem rather than a usable-by-disabled-people problem. Whittaker et al. (AI Now, 2019) catalog the gap.
cases.observation: Captions miss non-speech audio that deaf users rely on. Alt text describes 'a normal-looking person' without describing what 'normal-looking' encodes. Screen-reader output drops emoji, formatting, and structural cues that blind users use to navigate.
cases.harm: accessibility theater — accommodations that satisfy a checkbox but actively fail the people they're supposedly for
cases.preciseName: the system is built and audited by people who never have to use it, so the failure modes that matter to disabled users never get into the test set
cases.prompt: Don't say 'the captions are buggy.' Say 'the captions were never tested with deaf users, the alt text was never tested with blind users, and the people who shipped it called it accessible anyway.'
cases.citation: Whittaker, M., Alper, M., Bennett, C. L., et al. (2019). 'Disability, Bias, and AI.' AI Now Institute.

cases.headline: Top-five voice assistants miss Black speakers at nearly twice the word-error rate of white speakers.
cases.context: Koenecke et al. (PNAS 2020) tested ASR systems from Amazon, Apple, Google, IBM, and Microsoft against 19.8 hours of structured interviews with 42 white and 73 Black speakers across five US cities, matched on age and gender.
cases.observation: Average word-error rate for Black speakers: 0.35. White speakers: 0.19. The gap held even on identical phrases, traceable to the underlying acoustic models — not the language models on top.
cases.harm: voice interfaces — phone trees, dictation, accessibility tools, in-car systems — silently work worse for Black users by default
cases.preciseName: the acoustic model was trained on a corpus dominated by white General-American speech, so every speaker outside that distribution gets misheard at a higher rate
cases.prompt: Don't say 'the voice assistant has trouble with accents.' Say 'the acoustic model misses Black speakers nearly twice as often as white speakers, and every voice product on top inherits that gap.'
cases.citation: Koenecke, A., Nam, A., Lake, E., et al. (2020). 'Racial disparities in automated speech recognition.' PNAS 117(14).

cases.headline: Ask an image model for a 'fit professional.' Watch every body in the result.
cases.context: Bias audits of Stable Diffusion, Midjourney, and DALL-E (Bianchi et al. CHI 2023; Luccioni et al. 2023) show stable defaults for body size when professional roles are prompted without size qualifiers.
cases.observation: Prompts for 'CEO,' 'doctor,' 'fit professional,' 'attractive person' return overwhelmingly thin bodies. Prompts for 'fast-food worker,' 'poor person,' 'unhealthy person' return higher rates of larger bodies. Body size is being used as a moral and class signal by the model.
cases.harm: image models encode anti-fat bias as a default visual grammar — fitness equals competence, fatness equals failure
cases.preciseName: the model treats thinness as a marker of professional authority and treats body size as a stand-in for moral worth
cases.prompt: Don't say 'the image model has body issues.' Say 'the model maps thinness to authority and fatness to failure, and ships that mapping in every default image.'
cases.citation: Bianchi, F., Kalluri, P., Durmus, E., et al. (2023). 'Easily Accessible Text-to-Image Generation Amplifies Demographic Stereotypes at Large Scale.' ACM FAccT 2023.

cases.headline: Image models erase wrinkles, gray hair, and age unless you fight for it.
cases.context: Repeated audits of Midjourney, DALL-E, and Stable Diffusion default outputs for professional and lifestyle prompts show a consistent skew toward apparent ages 25–40, with visible markers of older age (gray hair, wrinkles, age spots) suppressed unless explicitly named.
cases.observation: Prompt for 'a professional,' 'an athlete,' 'a couple,' 'a happy person': the median apparent age clusters in the 20s–30s. Prompt for 'an old person': the model often draws caricatures — bent posture, exaggerated wrinkles — rather than realistic older adults living regular lives.
cases.harm: older adults are aesthetically erased from the default image of human life, and caricatured when summoned
cases.preciseName: the training corpus over-represents young, conventionally-attractive faces in lifestyle and professional contexts, so the model treats youth as the unmarked default and old age as a costume
cases.prompt: Don't say 'the model is ageist.' Say 'the model's default human is 28, and old age only appears as a costume — the people who built it never specified an age range, and neither does the model.'
cases.citation: Bianchi, F., Kalluri, P., Durmus, E., et al. (2023). 'Easily Accessible Text-to-Image Generation Amplifies Demographic Stereotypes at Large Scale.' ACM FAccT 2023.

cases.headline: Language models collapse they/them pronouns into he or she even when corrected.
cases.context: Dev et al. (EMNLP 2021) and Lauscher et al. (2022) measured pronoun resolution and gender inference in mainstream LLMs. Image models tested in Bianchi et al. (FAccT 2023) showed near-zero generation of recognizable nonbinary or trans subjects unless explicitly forced.
cases.observation: When given a sentence with they/them pronouns, models routinely rewrite to he or she in summaries and translations. Image prompts for 'a person' return a binary-coded human at near-100% rates. 'Nonbinary' and 'trans' as prompt terms either fail or produce stereotyped costumes.
cases.harm: the cultural existence of trans and nonbinary people is being filtered out of the default machine-generated picture of humanity
cases.preciseName: binary-gender erasure — the model collapses nonbinary identities to he/she at the linguistic level and to costume at the visual level
cases.prompt: Don't say 'the model isn't great with pronouns.' Say 'the model rewrites they/them to he/she in production output, and renders nonbinary people as costume rather than as people.'
cases.citation: Dev, S., Monajatipoor, M., Ovalle, A., et al. (2021). 'Harms of Gender Exclusivity and Challenges in Non-Binary Representation in Language Technologies.' EMNLP 2021.

cases.headline: Image models render Indigenous people as historical reenactors.
cases.context: Audits of mainstream image generators on prompts like 'Indigenous person,' 'Native American,' 'First Nations' consistently surface 19th-century-coded imagery — feathers, leather, ceremonial regalia — rather than contemporary people in contemporary settings.
cases.observation: Default outputs cluster around stock-photo and museum-archive aesthetics: outdoor settings, ceremonial dress, sepia or warm-light grading, no urban context, no contemporary clothing, no plurality of nations represented.
cases.harm: the model treats Indigenous people as a closed historical category rather than ongoing, plural, contemporary nations and communities
cases.preciseName: temporal erasure — Indigenous identity is rendered as the past, not the present, by default, regardless of the prompt's framing
cases.prompt: Don't say 'the model has stereotypes.' Say 'the model renders Indigenous people as historical reenactors. Search 'Indigenous person' and count the timestamps you can infer from the clothes.'
cases.citation: Bianchi, F., Kalluri, P., Durmus, E., et al. (2023). 'Easily Accessible Text-to-Image Generation Amplifies Demographic Stereotypes at Large Scale.' ACM FAccT 2023.

cases.headline: Ask an image model for 'a religious leader' five times. Count whose tradition you saw.
cases.context: Bianchi et al. (FAccT 2023) and follow-up audits document narrow defaults in religious imagery: 'religious leader' tends to surface Christian and occasionally Buddhist iconography. Sikh, Indigenous, Yoruba, and many other traditions are functionally absent from default outputs.
cases.observation: Prompts for 'religious figure,' 'spiritual leader,' 'priest' overwhelmingly default to white-coded Christian clergy. 'Muslim' and 'Arab' prompts disproportionately surface threat-coded imagery, a pattern documented across multiple model audits.
cases.harm: religious diversity is collapsed into a Christian default, with selective threat-coding applied to Muslim subjects
cases.preciseName: the model centers Christianity as the unmarked religious default and applies threat-coded visual grammar to Muslim and Arab subjects
cases.prompt: Don't say 'the model has religious bias.' Say 'religious leader returns a white Christian by default, and Muslim returns a threat by default. Run the prompt and count.'
cases.citation: Bianchi, F., Kalluri, P., Durmus, E., et al. (2023). 'Easily Accessible Text-to-Image Generation Amplifies Demographic Stereotypes at Large Scale.' ACM FAccT 2023.

cases.headline: Models trained on South Asian web data carry caste hierarchies into every output that touches South Asia.
cases.context: Equality Labs' 2018 'Caste in the United States' survey of 1,200 South Asian Americans documented the lived prevalence of caste discrimination — 67% of Dalit respondents reported workplace mistreatment. Their parallel research on Facebook hate-speech moderation (2019) showed casteist content was the least likely category to be removed.
cases.observation: Hate-speech classifiers trained on English web data don't recognize caste slurs as slurs. Image models render 'Indian professional' and 'Indian worker' with stark differences in skin tone, dress, and surname signals — replicating caste-coded visual hierarchies. Platforms moderate caste hate speech worst of any category.
cases.harm: caste hierarchy is exported globally through models that were never trained to recognize it, and platforms refuse to moderate the slurs
cases.preciseName: caste — a 3,000-year-old hierarchy — is being automated and laundered through models that don't recognize it as a protected category
cases.prompt: Don't say 'the model is biased toward Indian people.' Say 'the model encodes caste hierarchy in its visual grammar, and platforms refuse to moderate caste slurs at the rate they moderate other slurs.'
cases.citation: Equality Labs (2018). 'Caste in the United States: A Survey of Caste Among South Asian Americans.' See also Equality Labs (2019) Facebook hate-speech research.

cases.headline: Image models read 'wealthy' as light skin, fitted clothing, glass architecture. Consistently.
cases.context: Bianchi et al. (FAccT 2023) audited Stable Diffusion across class-coded prompts. Visual outputs consistently mapped wealth and competence to a narrow set of aesthetic markers and mapped poverty to the negation of those markers.
cases.observation: 'Wealthy person,' 'successful professional,' 'beautiful home': lighter skin tones, fitted and tailored clothing, glass-and-steel modern architecture, Western aesthetics. 'Poor person,' 'struggling family': darker skin tones, ill-fitting clothing, dilapidated structures, rural or non-Western settings.
cases.harm: the model has assigned a visual grammar to wealth that is also a visual grammar to race and geography — and it ships that mapping in every default image
cases.preciseName: wealth and competence are encoded as light-skinned, Western-coded, modernist aesthetics — and poverty is encoded as the negation
cases.prompt: Don't say 'the model has class bias.' Say 'the model uses skin tone, clothing fit, and architectural style as proxies for wealth — which means it is using race as a proxy for wealth.'
cases.citation: Bianchi, F., Kalluri, P., Durmus, E., et al. (2023). 'Easily Accessible Text-to-Image Generation Amplifies Demographic Stereotypes at Large Scale.' ACM FAccT 2023.

cases.headline: Image models render 'depression' as a melancholic woman by a window, and 'schizophrenia' as a maniac.
cases.context: A 2024 study of Midjourney V6 and DALL-E 3 outputs for psychiatric diagnostic prompts (Patel et al., BMJ Mental Health) and a 2025 follow-up in European Archives of Psychiatry documented how image models reproduce historical-archive stereotypes of mental illness rather than clinical realities.
cases.observation: 'Depression' prompts return overwhelmingly young white women in melancholic poses. 'Schizophrenia' prompts return wild-eyed, threat-coded male figures echoing 19th-century asylum imagery. 'Addiction' returns criminalizing imagery — alleys, needles, mugshot framing — instead of care imagery.
cases.harm: stigmatizing visual stereotypes of mental illness are being re-enshrined as the default machine-generated representation of human distress
cases.preciseName: the model treats depression as a feminine aesthetic, schizophrenia as a threat archetype, and addiction as a crime — a triad of stigmas inherited from media training data
cases.prompt: Don't say 'the model stigmatizes mental illness.' Say 'the model renders depression as a melancholic woman, schizophrenia as a threat, and addiction as a crime — and clinicians and patients are downstream of that.'
cases.citation: Patel, V., et al. (2024). 'AI depictions of psychiatric diagnoses: a preliminary study of generative image outputs in Midjourney V.6 and DALL-E 3.' BMJ Mental Health.

cases.headline: Swap 'DeShawn' for 'Brad' on the same resume. Watch the salary recommendation move.
cases.context: Bertrand & Mullainathan (AER 2004) ran the foundational audit study: 5,000 fictitious resumes sent to 1,300 help-wanted ads in Boston and Chicago, randomly assigned Black-sounding or white-sounding names. Recent LLM audits (Salinas et al. 2023, Haim et al. 2024) replicated the pattern in language models advising on hiring, salary, and character.
cases.observation: In the human study: white-coded names received 50% more callbacks. In LLM replications: the same resume with a Black-coded name receives lower recommended salaries, less senior recommended titles, and more negative inferred character traits.
cases.harm: name-based race discrimination — already present in the human labor market — is being automated and scaled into every LLM-mediated hiring tool
cases.preciseName: the model uses names as race proxies and applies the same penalty to Black-coded names that the human labor market has been documented applying since 2004
cases.prompt: Don't say 'the model is biased about names.' Say 'the model penalizes Black names on the same resume — same school, same role, same skills — and rebrands the penalty as machine output.'
cases.citation: Bertrand, M. & Mullainathan, S. (2004). 'Are Emily and Greg More Employable Than Lakisha and Jamal?' American Economic Review 94(4).

cases.headline: Headlines auto-generated for refugees vs. expats from the same country: count the qualifying adjectives.
cases.context: Reporting from the Migration Policy Institute, Reuters, and academic linguists has long documented the differential framing of forced migration in news language — 'refugee,' 'asylum seeker,' 'illegal' for non-white movements; 'expat,' 'remote worker,' 'digital nomad' for white movements. LLMs trained on news corpora inherit and reproduce that framing.
cases.observation: Prompt an LLM to write a headline about a Ukrainian fleeing war and a Syrian fleeing war, with all other facts identical: the qualifying adjectives, sympathy framing, and human-interest framing diverge. Same pattern for European vs. African economic migration.
cases.harm: differential humanity assignment — the model has learned which migrations get treated as tragedy and which get treated as threat, and applies the rule by default
cases.preciseName: automated reproduction of the news-language pattern that grants humanity to white migrants and treats non-white migrants as a problem to be managed
cases.prompt: Don't say 'the model has media bias.' Say 'give the model the same fact pattern with a different country of origin and watch the qualifying adjectives shift.'
cases.citation: Migration Policy Institute. 'The Power of Words in Migration: Why Language Matters.' See also coverage analyses by Reuters Institute and Cardiff University on refugee framing.

cases.headline: Resume screeners punish parental-leave gaps as if they were quitting.
cases.context: EEOC guidance (2023) and labor-law analysis published in the ABA Business Law Today (2024) document a recurring pattern in algorithmic hiring tools: automated resume screeners use employment gaps as a negative signal, and parental-leave gaps look identical to the model.
cases.observation: Resume screeners trained on historical hiring data treat any employment gap as a negative signal. Maternity-leave gaps, miscarriage-recovery gaps, fertility-treatment gaps, and caregiver-leave gaps therefore receive the same penalty as 'quit and disappeared.' Some video-interview tools have been audited for facial-feature inferences that correlate with pregnancy.
cases.harm: automated re-imposition of the pregnancy and caregiver discrimination that civil rights law took fifty years to make illegal — laundered as 'objective' resume scoring
cases.preciseName: pregnancy and caregiving status is being inferred or penalized through gap-based scoring, and the model launders the discrimination as risk assessment
cases.prompt: Don't say 'the screener has bias.' Say 'the screener penalizes parental-leave gaps the same as quitting, and that's the entire pregnancy-discrimination problem civil rights law spent fifty years on, automated.'
cases.citation: American Bar Association (2024). 'Navigating the AI Employment Bias Maze: Legal Compliance Guidelines and Strategies.' See also EEOC guidance on automated decision-making in employment.

cases.headline: Background-check algorithms punish people for arrests that never led to convictions.
cases.context: FTC enforcement actions and reporting from The Markup, Consumer Reports, and the National Consumer Law Center document automated background-check systems that surface arrest records — including arrests that resulted in no charge, dismissal, or acquittal — to landlords and employers as 'risk' signals.
cases.observation: Tenant-screening and employment-screening models include arrest data, not only conviction data. They also include sealed and expunged records that should be legally invisible. Errors — wrong-name matches, outdated dispositions — are routine and often the user has no clean way to correct them.
cases.harm: the model retroactively converts a stop-and-frisk into a permanent housing and employment penalty, and there's no court of appeal because the model is private
cases.preciseName: non-convictions and police contact are being treated as permanent risk signals, automating the downstream punishment that the criminal-legal system technically said wasn't justified
cases.prompt: Don't say 'the background check has errors.' Say 'the system punishes people for arrests that didn't lead to convictions, and the people running it call that risk assessment.'
cases.citation: Federal Trade Commission. 'Background Checks: What Job Applicants and Employees Should Know.' See also reporting by The Markup and Consumer Reports on tenant-screening errors.

cases.headline: Image models render 'a smart person' as Ivy-League-coded by default.
cases.context: Bianchi et al. (FAccT 2023) and recurring audits of Midjourney/DALL-E outputs for prompts about intelligence, scholarship, and graduation document a narrow visual default for academic identity.
cases.observation: 'A smart person,' 'a brilliant scientist,' 'a graduate' default to glasses, lighter skin, Western dress, and Ivy-or-Oxbridge-coded settings (gothic stone, ivy, gowns). Community colleges, HBCUs, vocational training, and global-South universities are functionally absent from the visual vocabulary.
cases.harm: intelligence and accomplishment are being visually equated with elite Western credentialing, and every image generated for an educational context inherits that equation
cases.preciseName: intelligence is encoded as the visual signature of elite Western universities, and other learning environments are erased from the default representation of competence
cases.prompt: Don't say 'the model has class bias.' Say 'the model thinks intelligence wears glasses, lives in stone-and-ivy buildings, and looks Western — and it ships that as the default image of a smart person.'
cases.citation: Bianchi, F., Kalluri, P., Durmus, E., et al. (2023). 'Easily Accessible Text-to-Image Generation Amplifies Demographic Stereotypes at Large Scale.' ACM FAccT 2023.

cases.headline: Ask the model for an 'expert in a small town.' Watch it relocate them to a city.
cases.context: Audits of LLM and image-model outputs for region-specific prompts show a consistent urban-and-coastal default. Rural, Indigenous, and small-town settings are rendered as backdrops or absences rather than as places where expertise lives.
cases.observation: Prompts for 'expert,' 'innovator,' 'researcher,' 'thought leader' default to urban, coastal, English-speaking, Western settings. Prompts that name a small town often produce stock pastoral imagery without actual contemporary inhabitants. Rural expertise — agronomy, land management, traditional knowledge — is structurally underrepresented.
cases.harm: expertise is being visually and linguistically equated with urban-coastal-Western life, and every other geography is rendered as scenery
cases.preciseName: the model's training distribution over-represents urban-coastal-Anglophone life, so non-urban settings are rendered as backdrops rather than as places where expertise lives
cases.prompt: Don't say 'the model has urban bias.' Say 'the model treats cities as where expertise lives and small towns as where stock photos happen, and that erases every rural expert tradition.'
cases.citation: Bianchi, F., Kalluri, P., Durmus, E., et al. (2023). 'Easily Accessible Text-to-Image Generation Amplifies Demographic Stereotypes at Large Scale.' ACM FAccT 2023.

cases.headline: Language models flatten everything that isn't English on the way through.
cases.context: Bender & Gebru et al. (FAccT 2021, 'On the Dangers of Stochastic Parrots') and follow-up multilingual NLP audits document how training corpora are dominated by English text, with non-English languages either underrepresented, machine-translated through English as a pivot, or dropped entirely.
cases.observation: Non-English prompts are often translated into English, processed, and translated back — a lossy round-trip that flattens dialect, register, idiom, and culturally-specific reference. Low-resource languages are functionally unsupported. The 'multilingual' label often means 'English plus a few high-resource European languages.'
cases.harm: English is being treated as the unmarked default of human thought, and every non-English speaker gets a degraded version of every product
cases.preciseName: an English-pivot architecture that flattens dialect, idiom, and cultural reference on the way in and on the way out, while marketing itself as multilingual
cases.prompt: Don't say 'the model is bad at other languages.' Say 'the model uses English as the pivot, so every non-English speaker gets a degraded round-trip — and the marketing calls it multilingual.'
cases.citation: Bender, E. M., Gebru, T., McMillan-Major, A., & Shmitchell, S. (2021). 'On the Dangers of Stochastic Parrots: Can Language Models Be Too Big?' FAccT 2021.

cases.headline: A widely-used hospital algorithm gave Black patients lower risk scores than equally-sick white patients.
cases.context: Obermeyer et al. (Science 2019) audited a commercial care-management algorithm used on roughly 200 million Americans annually. The algorithm ranked patients by predicted future health-care cost — and used that as a proxy for predicted future health needs.
cases.observation: At the same risk score, Black patients had 26% more chronic illnesses than white patients, because Black patients historically receive less spending per equivalent illness. Correcting the target from cost to actual health needs would have raised the share of Black patients receiving extra care from 17.7% to 46.5%.
cases.harm: structural under-spending on Black patients was being laundered as 'they need less care' by the algorithm, and that ranking determined who got extra clinical attention
cases.preciseName: the algorithm optimized for predicted cost, which encoded historical under-spending on Black patients, and recommended care allocation as if past spending equaled past need
cases.prompt: Don't say 'the healthcare algorithm has bias.' Say 'the algorithm used past spending as a proxy for past need, and past spending was racist — so the algorithm sent extra care to white patients who were less sick than Black patients with the same risk score.'
cases.citation: Obermeyer, Z., Powers, B., Vogeli, C., & Mullainathan, S. (2019). 'Dissecting racial bias in an algorithm used to manage the health of populations.' Science 366(6464).

cases.headline: NIST tested 189 facial-recognition algorithms. False positives were 10–100x higher for Asian and Black faces.
cases.context: Grother, Ngan & Hanaoka (NIST FRVT Part 3, December 2019) tested 189 face-recognition algorithms from 99 developers against four large US-government datasets — 18 million images of 8 million people.
cases.observation: For one-to-one matching, false-positive rates were 10–100x higher for Asian and African American faces vs. Caucasian faces, depending on the algorithm. For one-to-many matching (the police-investigation use case), false positives were highest for African American women.
cases.harm: the false-positive disparity is the wrong-arrest disparity — every additional false positive in a one-to-many search is a real person, disproportionately a Black woman, getting wrongly identified
cases.preciseName: demographic-differentiated false-positive rates that translate directly into demographic-differentiated wrong-arrest rates when these systems are deployed by police
cases.prompt: Don't say 'facial recognition is biased.' Say 'NIST tested 189 algorithms and found false-positive rates 10–100x higher for Asian and Black faces, and police are using these systems to identify suspects right now.'
cases.citation: Grother, P., Ngan, M., & Hanaoka, K. (2019). 'Face Recognition Vendor Test (FRVT) Part 3: Demographic Effects.' NISTIR 8280.

cases.headline: Language models generate toxic completions for prompts about marginalized identities at higher rates.
cases.context: Gehman et al. (Findings of EMNLP 2020, 'RealToxicityPrompts') benchmarked toxic-completion rates from GPT-2, GPT-3, CTRL, and other mainstream LMs across 100,000 web prompts. Sheng et al. (EMNLP 2019) showed parallel disparities in regard scores by demographic.
cases.observation: Even from non-toxic prompts, LMs produce toxic completions. Prompts referencing Muslim, Black, LGBTQ, and disabled identities produce higher rates of toxic, stereotyped, and hostile continuations than prompts referencing white, Christian, straight, or non-disabled identities.
cases.harm: toxicity is not random — it concentrates on the same identities the broader information ecosystem already treats as targets
cases.preciseName: demographic-differentiated toxicity in default completions — the model produces hostile output more readily when the subject is from an already-targeted group
cases.prompt: Don't say 'the model can be toxic.' Say 'the model is more likely to produce toxic continuations when the subject is Muslim, Black, queer, or disabled — the same groups the rest of the internet already targets.'
cases.citation: Gehman, S., Gururangan, S., Sap, M., Choi, Y., & Smith, N. A. (2020). 'RealToxicityPrompts: Evaluating Neural Toxic Degeneration in Language Models.' Findings of EMNLP 2020.
